title: Inter-Control-Center Link Availability from Synthetic Tests

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing to partial: UC provides meaningful monitoring evidence for CIP-012-1 R2 Part 2.1.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NERC CIP CIP-012-1 R2 Part 2.1 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.13.62: Inter-Control-Center Link Availability from Synthetic Tests.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.13.62' running on index=synthetic sourcetype=http:ping or sourcetype=tcp:check, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=synthetic (sourcetype=http:ping OR sourcetype=tcp:check) cip012_link=1 earliest=-24h
| stats count(eval(match(status,"(?i)success"))) as ok count as total by link_id
| eval pct=round(100*ok/total,2)
| where pct < 99.5
| lookup control_center_links.csv link_id OUTPUT src_cc dest_cc
| table link_id, src_cc, dest_cc, pct, ok, total
description: We measure synthetic ping or application handshake success rates between control centers to evidence communications availability.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NERC CIP compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes synthetic and sourcetypes http:ping, http:ping`, tcp:check. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Deploy lightweight synthetic from each center. (2) Tune SLA percentage. (3) Page network operations on breach. (4) Retain raw samples for monthly report. Indexes required: `index=synthetic`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=http:ping`, `sourcetype=http:ping``, `sourcetype=tcp:check`, `sourcetype=tcp:check``; Lookups: `control_center_links`, `control_center_links.csv`.
visualization: Time chart (availability), Single value (worst link), Table (breaches). Key fields: `dest_cc`, `link_id`, `ok`, `pct`, `src_cc`, `total`.
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=synthetic` `sourcetype=http:ping` or `sourcetype=tcp:check`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Deploy lightweight synthetic from each center. (2) Tune SLA percentage. (3) Page network operations on breach. (4) Retain raw samples for monthly report.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=synthetic (sourcetype=http:ping OR sourcetype=tcp:check) cip012_link=1 earliest=-24h
| stats count(eval(match(status,"(?i)success"))) as ok count as total by link_id
| eval pct=round(100*ok/total,2)
| where pct < 99.5
| lookup control_center_links.csv link_id OUTPUT src_cc dest_cc
| table link_id, src_cc, dest_cc, pct, ok, total
```

Understanding this SPL

**Inter-Control-Center Link Availability from Synthetic Tests (CIP-012-1 R2 Part 2.1)** — We measure synthetic ping or application handshake success rates between control centers to evidence communications availability.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=synthetic` `sourcetype=http:ping` or `sourcetype=tcp:check`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: synthetic; **sourcetype**: http:ping, tcp:check. Those sourcetypes align with what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=synthetic, sourcetype=http:ping, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` aggregates events by **link_id**, producing one row per unique combination for trend analysis and threshold evaluation.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **pct** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Filters the current rows with `where pct < 99.5` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).

Output fields: dest_cc, link_id, ok, pct, src_cc, total.

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Time chart (availability), Single value (worst link), Table (breaches)
grandmaExplanation: We watch for we measure synthetic ping or application handshake success rates between control centers to evidence communications availability. This is critical because gaps here could mean serious regulatory exposure or security blind spots.
